text:
COMPREHENSIVE MYELOID NEXT-GENERATION SEQUENCING REPORT

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PATIENT INFORMATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Patient Name: Margaret Chen
Date of Birth: 1977
Sex: Female
MRN: [REDACTED]
Ordering Physician: [Hematology/Oncology Attending]
Clinical Indication: Newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia (AML), NOS — comprehensive molecular profiling for risk stratification and therapeutic guidance
Specimen Type: Bone marrow aspirate (diagnostic)
Specimen Collection Site: Right posterior iliac crest
Specimen ID: [LAB-ID REDACTED]
Tumor Content (estimated): 72% myeloblasts by concurrent morphology
Test Performed: Comprehensive Myeloid NGS Panel — 523-gene panel with integrated FLT3-ITD detection, copy number analysis, and fusion transcript profiling
Sequencing Platform: Illumina NovaSeq 6000
Mean Coverage Depth: 1,124x (hotspot regions up to 3,000x)
Analytical Sensitivity: SNVs/indels ≥ 3% VAF; FLT3-ITD ≥ 1% allelic ratio; copy number alterations ≥ 30% clonal fraction

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CLINICAL SUMMARY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
This 48-year-old female presented with de novo acute myeloid leukemia with leukocytosis (WBC 87,400/uL), severe anemia (hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL), thrombocytopenia (platelets 32,000/uL), gingival hyperplasia, and circulating blasts (~40%) on peripheral smear. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed hypercellular marrow (95% cellularity) with 72% myeloblasts and Auer rods. Immunophenotype by flow cytometry: CD34+, CD117+, CD13+, CD33+, MPO+, HLA-DR+, CD7 dim, CD19-, CD3- — consistent with AML, NOS. Conventional karyotype: 46,XX (normal female karyotype, 20/20 metaphases). FISH panel negative for t(8;21), inv(16), and t(15;17). This NGS study was performed on the diagnostic bone marrow aspirate specimen for comprehensive molecular profiling.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SOMMARY OF RESULTS — CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

FINDING 1 — PATHOGENIC / CLINICALLY ACTIONABLE
Gene: FLT3
Alteration: Internal Tandem Duplication (ITD), Exon 14
Nucleotide Change: 33-base pair in-frame insertion within the juxtamembrane domain (c.1771_1772ins33)
Protein Effect: In-frame insertion, juxtamembrane domain
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 40.2%
FLT3-ITD Allelic Ratio: 0.68 (HIGH; threshold for high allelic ratio: ≥0.5)
Classification: Pathogenic
Clinical Significance: FLT3-ITD mutations occur in approximately 25-30% of AML cases and are among the most clinically impactful alterations in AML. This mutation results in constitutive, ligand-independent activation of the FLT3 receptor tyrosine kinase, driving proliferative signaling through RAS/MAPK, PI3K/AKT, and STAT5 pathways. A high FLT3-ITD allelic ratio (≥0.5) is independently associated with inferior complete remission rates, higher relapse risk, and significantly worse overall survival compared to low allelic ratio. In the NPM1-unmutated setting (as observed here), high-allelic-ratio FLT3-ITD confers Adverse risk classification per ELN 2022 guidelines. This mutation also predicts for clinical benefit from FLT3 inhibitor therapy; midostaurin (RATIFY trial, NEJM 2017) demonstrated improved event-free and overall survival when added to standard 7+3 induction in FLT3-mutated AML.
Note: FLT3 tyrosine kinase domain (TKD) mutation D835 was NOT detected (see negative findings). Co-occurrence with DNMT3A and TET2 (see below) defines a leukemogenic cluster associated with myeloid transformation.

FINDING 2 — PATHOGENIC
Gene: DNMT3A
Alteration: Missense mutation
Nucleotide Change: c.2645G>A
Protein Change: p.Arg882His (R882H)
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 48.1%
Chromosomal Location: Chr2q23.3
Classification: Pathogenic
Clinical Significance: The DNMT3A p.R882H hotspot mutation is the most frequent DNMT3A alteration in AML, accounting for approximately 60% of all DNMT3A mutations in this disease. DNMT3A encodes a de novo DNA methyltransferase critical for epigenetic regulation of hematopoietic gene expression. The R882H mutation acts through a dominant-negative mechanism, reducing DNMT3A enzymatic activity by approximately 80% and resulting in focal hypomethylation of CpG sites, particularly at hematopoietic stem cell-associated loci (e.g., HOX gene clusters). DNMT3A R882H is found at high VAF (~50%), consistent with its role as an early or founder event. Importantly, this mutation has been described as a CHIP (clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential) lesion and can persist as a pre-leukemic clone; its presence in the context of AML carries adverse prognostic implications. Co-mutation with FLT3-ITD is well-documented and synergistic: DNMT3A R882H establishes an epigenetically permissive state that potentiates FLT3-driven leukemic transformation. The high VAF (48.1%) suggests this mutation resides in the founding clone or a very early branch. Of note, this mutation is frequently detectable even in morphologic remission and does not constitute MRD positivity per current consensus.

FINDING 3 — PATHOGENIC
Gene: RUNX1
Alteration: Frameshift mutation
Nucleotide Change: c.871dupA (duplication leading to frameshift)
Protein Change: p.Ser291fs*16 (S291fs*16)
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 43.7%
Chromosomal Location: Chr21q22.12
Classification: Pathogenic
Clinical Significance: RUNX1 (Runt-related transcription factor 1; formerly AML1) encodes the alpha subunit of the CBF (core binding factor) transcription complex, which is essential for definitive hematopoiesis. This frameshift insertion results in loss of the transactivation domain and premature termination at position 306, generating a truncated protein predicted to function as a dominant-negative inhibitor of the remaining wild-type RUNX1 allele. RUNX1 mutations are found in approximately 10-15% of AML cases and carry significant prognostic weight. Under ELN 2022 classification, RUNX1 mutations confer Adverse risk when present without NPM1 mutation (as in this patient). RUNX1 mutations impair myeloid differentiation and augment self-renewal of leukemic progenitors. The co-occurrence of RUNX1 mutation with FLT3-ITD (high allelic ratio) and DNMT3A R882H in an NPM1-unmutated background constitutes a particularly high-risk molecular profile. The VAF of 43.7% is consistent with a clonal mutation in the dominant leukemic clone, potentially present in most blast cells given the high blast burden. This combination has been associated with primary resistance to standard induction chemotherapy and underscores the rationale for allogeneic stem cell transplantation in first complete remission.

FINDING 4 — PATHOGENIC
Gene: TET2
Alteration: Nonsense mutation
Nucleotide Change: c.2746C>T
Protein Change: p.Gln916* (Q916*)
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 22.4%
Chromosomal Location: Chr4q24
Classification: Pathogenic
Clinical Significance: TET2 encodes a dioxygenase that catalyzes the conversion of 5-methylcytosine (5mC) to 5-hydroxymethylcytosine (5hmC), an essential step in the active DNA demethylation pathway. This nonsense mutation at codon 916 introduces a premature stop codon, resulting in loss of TET2 enzymatic function and abnormal DNA hypermethylation at target loci. TET2 mutations are highly recurrent in both CHIP and myeloid malignancies; they are found in approximately 7-23% of AML cases. The lower VAF (22.4%) compared to the FLT3-ITD (40.2%) and DNMT3A (48.1%) alleles suggests this mutation is likely subclonal — present in a subset of leukemic cells and potentially representing late clonal evolution or a minor subclone. Alternatively, loss of heterozygosity at 4q24 could modulate the apparent VAF. TET2 loss-of-function cooperates with DNMT3A deficiency to create additive epigenetic dysregulation in leukemic stem cells. TET2 mutations have also been linked to hypersensitivity to hypomethylating agent therapy; this information may be relevant for future treatment decisions in the event of relapse.

FINDING 5 — VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS)
Gene: WT1
Alteration: Missense mutation
Nucleotide Change: c.1385G>A
Protein Change: p.Arg462Gln (R462Q)
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 18.9%
Chromosomal Location: Chr11p13
Classification: Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS)
Clinical Significance: WT1 (Wilms Tumor 1) encodes a zinc finger transcription factor involved in hematopoietic differentiation. WT1 mutations occur in approximately 8-10% of AML cases, predominantly as missense or frameshift mutations in exons 7 and 9. The p.R462Q substitution affects the zinc finger DNA-binding domain (ZF4); functional studies of this specific variant are limited, which precludes definitive pathogenicity classification at this time. WT1 mutations have been reported to be associated with adverse outcomes in normal-karyotype AML and may be associated with resistance to standard chemotherapy, though findings across studies are heterogeneous. WT1 overexpression (separate from mutation) is used as a quantitative MRD marker in AML. The subclonal VAF (18.9%) places this mutation in a late subclone. Correlation with other molecular findings and clinical context is recommended.

FINDING 6 — LIKELY PATHOGENIC
Gene: PTPN11
Alteration: Missense mutation
Nucleotide Change: c.226G>A
Protein Change: p.Glu76Lys (E76K)
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 8.3%
Chromosomal Location: Chr12q24.13
Classification: Likely Pathogenic
Clinical Significance: PTPN11 encodes SHP-2 (Src homology-2 domain-containing protein tyrosine phosphatase-2), a positive regulator of the RAS/MAPK signaling pathway. The p.E76K mutation is a recurrent gain-of-function mutation that activates SHP-2, resulting in enhanced RAS-MAPK and PI3K-AKT signaling. PTPN11 activating mutations are more commonly associated with juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia (JMML) and Noonan syndrome but are found in approximately 2-5% of AML cases. This mutation is present at a low VAF (8.3%), consistent with a minor subclone, possibly representing late clonal evolution or minor resistant cell population. RAS pathway activation through PTPN11 may contribute to proliferative signaling and could represent a cooperative lesion with FLT3-ITD. Notably, no NRAS or KRAS mutations were identified (see negative findings below), making this the sole RAS pathway activating event in this specimen.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SUMMARY TABLE — SOMATIC MUTATIONS DETECTED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Gene      | Alteration              | VAF / AR   | Classification    | ELN 2022 Impact
----------|-------------------------|------------|-------------------|----------------
FLT3      | ITD, ex14, 33bp ins     | AR 0.68    | Pathogenic        | Adverse (high AR, NPM1-unmut)
DNMT3A    | p.R882H                 | 48.1%      | Pathogenic        | Adverse (contextual)
RUNX1     | p.S291fs*16             | 43.7%      | Pathogenic        | Adverse
TET2      | p.Q916*                 | 22.4%      | Pathogenic        | Adverse (contextual)
WT1       | p.R462Q                 | 18.9%      | VUS               | Unknown
PTPN11    | p.E76K                  | 8.3%       | Likely Pathogenic | Informational

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
NEGATIVE FINDINGS — CLINICALLY RELEVANT GENES TESTED AND NOT MUTATED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

The following clinically relevant targets were tested and returned NEGATIVE (no pathogenic or likely pathogenic alterations detected):

- FLT3-TKD (D835, I836): NOT DETECTED — absence of TKD mutation is relevant; TKD mutations may confer differential sensitivity to FLT3 inhibitors (e.g., quizartinib has reduced activity against TKD mutations relative to ITD)
- NPM1 (Nucleophosmin 1, exon 12 mutations): NOT DETECTED — NPM1 unmutated; in the context of FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, NPM1-unmutated status defines Adverse ELN risk. Had NPM1 been co-mutated with FLT3-ITD, the risk category would be Intermediate per ELN 2022.
- CEBPA: NOT DETECTED — no single or biallelic CEBPA mutations identified
- IDH1 (R132): NOT DETECTED
- IDH2 (R140, R172): NOT DETECTED — absence of IDH1/IDH2 mutations precludes eligibility for IDH inhibitor monotherapy
- TP53: NOT DETECTED — TP53 wild-type; absence associated with relatively better response to standard induction compared to TP53-mutated AML
- ASXL1: NOT DETECTED
- ASXL2: NOT DETECTED
- EZH2: NOT DETECTED
- NRAS (G12, G13, Q61): NOT DETECTED
- KRAS (G12, G13, Q61): NOT DETECTED
- KIT (D816, exon 8): NOT DETECTED
- CBL: NOT DETECTED
- NF1: NOT DETECTED
- BRAF: NOT DETECTED
- JAK2 (V617F and exon 12): NOT DETECTED
- MPL: NOT DETECTED
- CALR: NOT DETECTED
- GATA2: NOT DETECTED
- GATA1: NOT DETECTED
- PHF6: NOT DETECTED
- BCOR: NOT DETECTED
- BCORL1: NOT DETECTED
- RAD21: NOT DETECTED
- SMC1A / SMC3 / STAG2 (cohesin complex): NOT DETECTED
- SF3B1 / SRSF2 / U2AF1 / ZRSR2 (splicing factor genes): NOT DETECTED
- DDX41: NOT DETECTED (germline evaluation separately recommended given de novo presentation; however, no DDX41 variant identified in this tumor specimen)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
COPY NUMBER ANALYSIS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
No significant somatic copy number alterations (SCNAs) detected. No gains or losses meeting threshold criteria (>30% clonal fraction) were identified across all 523 genes on this panel. In particular:
- No deletion of 5q, 7q, or 17p detected
- No trisomy 8 detected
- No deletion of TP53 locus (17p13.1) detected
- No amplification of MYC (8q24), MECOM (3q26), or FLT3 (13q12) detected

This copy number neutral profile is concordant with the conventional cytogenetic result of 46,XX (normal karyotype, 20/20 metaphases).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FUSION TRANSCRIPT ANALYSIS (RNA-based / DNA-level breakpoint analysis)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
No pathognomonic or clinically significant gene fusions detected.

Specifically, the following recurrent AML-associated fusions were evaluated and NOT detected:
- RUNX1-RUNX1T1 [t(8;21)(q22;q22)]: NEGATIVE
- CBFB-MYH11 [inv(16)(p13.1q22) / t(16;16)]: NEGATIVE
- PML-RARA [t(15;17)(q24;q21)]: NEGATIVE
- BCR-ABL1 [t(9;22)(q34;q11)]: NEGATIVE
- KMT2A (MLL) rearrangements (KMT2A-AFF1, KMT2A-MLLT3, KMT2A-MLLT10, and 14 additional partners): NEGATIVE
- NUP98 rearrangements (NUP98-NSD1, NUP98-HOXA9, NUP98-KDM5A): NEGATIVE
- DEK-NUP214 [t(6;9)]: NEGATIVE
- RBM15-MKL1 [t(1;22)]: NEGATIVE
- ETV6-RUNX1: NEGATIVE
- FUS-ERG: NEGATIVE

Note: The presence of RUNX1 frameshift mutation (Finding 3) represents a somatic point mutation/indel in the RUNX1 gene, distinct from and not to be confused with the RUNX1-RUNX1T1 fusion arising from t(8;21), which was confirmed negative.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
MRD MARKER IDENTIFICATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
The following mutations are suitable for quantitative MRD monitoring by highly sensitive assays (e.g., ddPCR or quantitative PCR) at subsequent time points:

1. FLT3-ITD (33 bp insertion, allelic ratio 0.68) — highly specific for leukemic clone; quantitative PCR-based MRD assay applicable; sensitivity 0.001%
2. DNMT3A p.R882H — ddPCR or amplicon-based deep sequencing applicable; NOTE: DNMT3A mutations can persist in pre-leukemic CHIP clones in remission and should be interpreted with caution as a standalone MRD marker; co-assessment with FLT3-ITD recommended
3. RUNX1 p.S291fs*16 — leukemia-specific frameshift; NGS deep sequencing MRD monitoring applicable; sensitivity <0.1% VAF
4. TET2 p.Q916* — subclonal at diagnosis (VAF 22.4%); may be less informative as MRD marker due to potential persistence as CHIP; monitor in context

Primary recommended MRD markers: FLT3-ITD and RUNX1 p.S291fs*16 (leukemia-specific)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CLONAL ARCHITECTURE INTERPRETATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Inferred clonal hierarchy based on VAF and tumor content:

Founding / Early Clone (VAF ~45-50%):  DNMT3A p.R882H (VAF 48.1%)
  └─ Primary Leukemic Clone (VAF ~40-44%):  FLT3-ITD (AR 0.68 / VAF 40.2%) + RUNX1 p.S291fs*16 (VAF 43.7%)
       └─ Subclone 1 (VAF ~22%):  TET2 p.Q916* (VAF 22.4%)
            └─ Subclone 2 (VAF ~19%):  WT1 p.R462Q (VAF 18.9%)
       └─ Minor Subclone (VAF ~8%):  PTPN11 p.E76K (VAF 8.3%)

Interpretation: DNMT3A R882H likely represents the initiating or pre-leukemic event (consistent with CHIP origin), upon which FLT3-ITD and RUNX1 mutations co-occurred to drive frank AML transformation. TET2, WT1, and PTPN11 mutations represent secondary events in subclonal evolution. The presence of multiple subclones with RAS pathway (PTPN11) and epigenetic (TET2) alterations suggests significant clonal complexity and potential for selective pressure during therapy.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ELN 2022 RISK STRATIFICATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Overall ELN 2022 Risk Classification: ADVERSE

Rationale:
- FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio (≥0.5) in an NPM1-unmutated background → Adverse
- RUNX1 frameshift mutation (without NPM1 co-mutation) → Adverse
- Normal karyotype (46,XX) does not independently modify risk in this molecular context
- DNMT3A R882H adds contextual adverse risk through its epigenetic destabilizing effect

Note: Per ELN 2022, if NPM1 mutation had been present alongside FLT3-ITD (regardless of allelic ratio), the risk classification could be reclassified as Intermediate. The absence of NPM1 mutation is therefore an important negative finding with direct prognostic and therapeutic implications.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
QUALITY CONTROL METRICS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Specimen Adequacy: Adequate
Estimated tumor cellularity: 72% (blasts by morphology; confirms adequate tumor DNA content for variant detection)
Total sequencing reads (on-target): 98.4%
Mean depth of coverage: 1,124x
FLT3-ITD coverage (dedicated assay region): 3,180x
Uniformity (% bases ≥ 0.2x mean): 97.8%
Germline contamination control: Pass
Sequencing QC: All quality metrics within acceptable parameters. This result is considered reportable.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
METHODOLOGY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
DNA and RNA were extracted from bone marrow aspirate using standard protocols. Library preparation utilized a hybrid-capture approach covering all exons of 523 clinically relevant hematologic malignancy genes, as well as intronic sequences spanning known fusion breakpoints. FLT3-ITD detection employed a dedicated amplicon-based high-depth sequencing assay with fragment length analysis as an orthogonal confirmatory method. Allelic ratio for FLT3-ITD was calculated as ITD-containing reads divided by total (WT + ITD) reads. Variant calling employed validated bioinformatics pipelines with matched germline reference (buccal swab). Somatic variants were confirmed as somatic by absence in matched normal. All variants reported at VAF ≥ 3% in tumor regions with coverage ≥ 500x. Copy number analysis was performed using normalization against a panel of normals. Fusion detection utilized both DNA-level breakpoint capture and RNA-seq-based junction read analysis.

This report was generated in conjunction with the concurrent bone marrow morphology, flow cytometry, cytogenetics (conventional karyotype and FISH), and clinical data. Integration of all diagnostic modalities is required for complete AML diagnosis and risk stratification per WHO 2022 and ELN 2022 guidelines.
